title: LA-1: Solo and Co-op Cyberpunk Detective Game (Overview and Review)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-Fkj0aK2dO8

Daniel (@dungeondive) absolutely loves LA-1, a cyberpunk detective mystery game from Richard Loomis that reminds him of Fantasy Flight Games' golden age. This isn't a deduction puzzle for the player to solve—it's a mystery game where characters investigate a case through narrative experience. You're playing detectives working for Mason-Doyle Investigations in a post-apocalyptic Los Angeles, solving cases across three zones: the elite City of Angels, old Los Angeles, and the gritty Underworld.

The design is remarkably elegant. With only 12 pages of core rules, gameplay flows smoothly through darkness phases (mythos-style pressure), action phases, and investigation phases. The deck-building system is subtle: equipment provides passive bonuses, and stat cards from your hand let you attempt challenges while managing heat (visibility) and damage. There are 800+ encounter cards with multiple versions of each, meaning the same case plays wildly differently each time. Daniel played it three times in one week because the theme, atmosphere, and narrative-driven mystery system scratched an itch he hadn't felt in years.

The main caveat? The oversized board is a serious table hog, and missing card dividers in a 800-card library is puzzling. But neither flaw dampens his enthusiasm for what might be the best cooperative detective game in years.

----

Does the shift toward character-driven investigation rather than player deduction feel more satisfying to you in mystery games?
